18.1.title: Arjuna's twin question: saṃnyāsa and tyāga
18.1.body: Arjuna opens the final chapter with षष्ठी genitives — संन्यासस्य and त्यागस्य — both depending on तत्त्वम् ("the truth/essence [of]"), the द्वितीया object of वेदितुम् (infinitive of √विद्, "to know"). The particle पृथक् ("separately") is the crux: he has heard both terms used through the Gītā and now wants their tattva drawn apart, not collapsed.

18.1.title: Three vocatives for one Krishna
18.1.body: महाबाहो, हृषीकेश, केशिनिषूदन — three सम्बोधन epithets stacked in two lines mark the gravity of the closing inquiry. Each addresses a different facet: the strong-armed protector, the master of the senses (the relevant power for तत्त्व-grasping), and the slayer of the demon Keśin (the destroyer of inner obstacles).

18.2.title: Saṃnyāsa as renunciation of desire-driven action
18.2.body: Krishna defines the terms via two parallel constructions. काम्यानां कर्मणां न्यासम् (षष्ठी + द्वितीया) — "the laying-down of desire-prompted actions" — is what the कवयः call saṃnyāsa. The verb विदुः (√विद्, लट्, बहुवचन) carries the weight of received tradition.

18.2.title: Tyāga as renunciation of fruits, not action
18.2.body: सर्वकर्मफलत्यागम् is a tatpuruṣa compound: the abandoning of the fruits of all actions. प्राहुः (perfect of √ब्रू, "they have declared") names the विचक्षणाः — the discerning — as authority. The distinction is fine: saṃnyāsa drops काम्य actions; tyāga drops फल across the board while keeping the act.

18.3.title: Two rival schools framed in एके / अपरे
18.3.body: The verse stages a debate. त्याज्यं दोषवत् — "to be abandoned as flawed" — is the view of एके मनीषिणः. यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यम् is the rival view of अपरे. The indeclinable इति marks each as a quoted position, with Krishna remaining the arbiter.

18.3.title: दोषवत् — the suffix that means "like a flaw"
18.3.body: दोषवत् uses the तद्धित suffix वति (Pā. 5.1.115, "like, as"), attached to दोष — distinct from मतुप् (दोषवान् = "possessing fault"). The first school treats action itself as inherently faulty (like the Sāṃkhyas / extreme renunciants); the second protects the trio यज्ञ-दान-तप as purifying. The seeming contradiction is what 18.4-6 will resolve.

18.4.title: निश्चयं शृणु — Krishna asserts the verdict
18.4.body: Two vocatives — भरतसत्तम, पुरुषव्याघ्र — frame Krishna's decisive intervention. निश्चयम् ("the settled conclusion") is द्वितीया, object of शृणु (लोट्, मध्यम पुरुष, एकवचन). The threefold त्याग (त्रिविधः सम्प्रकीर्तितः) prepared in 7-9 mirrors the threefold guṇa-classification that will run through the chapter.

18.4.title: सम्प्रकीर्तितः — declared with full emphasis
18.4.body: सम्प्रकीर्तितः is a कृदन्त (past passive participle) of सम् + प्र + √कीर्त्, agreeing with त्यागः (प्रथमा एकवचन पुल्लिङ्ग). The double prefix intensifies: it is not merely said but proclaimed at length — Krishna positions the upcoming guṇa-analysis as authoritative śāstric pronouncement.

18.5.title: कार्यमेव — the emphatic gerundive
18.5.body: न त्याज्यम् and कार्यम् are both potential passives (कृत्य suffixes -तव्य, -य): "not to be abandoned," "to be done." The एव after कार्यम् pins the verdict — sacrifice, charity, austerity must be done. पावनानि (नपुंसक बहुवचन, agreeing with the implied three actions) names them as purifiers of the discerning.

18.5.title: मनीषिणाम् — षष्ठी of beneficiary
18.5.body: मनीषिणाम् is षष्ठी बहुवचन — "of/for the wise." The purifying trio is not a path for the unprepared but the daily discipline of those already on the path; पावनानि is नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन because यज्ञ-दान-तप are taken as a collective neuter triad.

18.6.title: सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलानि च — the sāttvika test
18.6.body: त्यक्त्वा is the absolutive (क्त्वा-pratyaya, no prefix on the root) — "having abandoned attachment and fruits." The two द्वितीया objects, सङ्गम् and फलानि, name what must go before the action proceeds. The action stays; the inner posture changes. This is the sāttvika tyāga 18.9 will codify.

18.6.title: मे मतमुत्तमम् — Krishna stamps the verdict
18.6.body: मतम् (कृदन्त of √मन्) takes षष्ठी मे: "my settled view, the highest." The कर्तव्यानि (gerundive, नपुंसक बहुवचन) refers back to the यज्ञ-दान-तप triad. Note the position: Krishna places his own decisive उत्तम मत before unveiling the threefold tyāga analysis.

18.7.title: Tāmasa tyāga — abandoning the obligatory
18.7.body: नियतस्य कर्मणः (षष्ठी एकवचन) means "of obligatory action." नोपपद्यते (न + उपपद्यते, √पद् + उप, लट्) — "is not fitting." Renouncing prescribed duty out of मोह is parikīrtita (declared) as tāmasa: the lowest of the three tyāgas because it has confusion at its root.

18.7.title: मोहात् — पञ्चमी of cause
18.7.body: मोहात् is पञ्चमी एकवचन, expressing the cause: "out of delusion." Krishna's grammar isolates the diagnostic — what makes a tyāga tāmasa is not the act of dropping but the cognitive state from which the dropping issues.

18.8.title: Rājasa tyāga — abandoning out of bodily fear
18.8.body: कायक्लेशभयात् is a long तत्पुरुष in पञ्चमी: "from fear of bodily distress." त्यजेत् is विधिलिङ् (potential, "should one abandon") — Krishna names the hypothetical. The result clause uses optative लभेत् too: such a one "would not obtain the fruit of tyāga." Effort spent, reward forfeited.

18.8.title: दुःखमिति — the labeling that disqualifies
18.8.body: The indeclinable इति quotes the inner verdict: "thinking, 'this is suffering.'" The rājasa tyāgī labels the work itself as misery and walks away. Note the subtlety — rejecting work as painful is itself a rājasa stance, not a sāttvika discernment.

18.9.title: Sāttvika tyāga — duty done, fruit released
18.9.body: कार्यमिति — "thinking, 'this is to be done'" — is the inner posture of the sāttvika. क्रियते is passive लट् ("is done"), making the action impersonal: it gets done because it must, not because the doer needs it. सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलं चैव — attachment and fruit are both released.

18.9.title: The triad climax — मतः
18.9.body: मतः (कृदन्त of √मन्, प्रथमा एकवचन पुल्लिङ्ग) agrees with त्यागः. After codifying tāmasa (7), rājasa (8), Krishna pronounces the third with deliberate stillness: "that tyāga is held to be sāttvika." This is the definition that will radiate through 18.23, 18.26, 18.33, 18.37.

18.10.title: Five marks of the त्यागी
18.10.body: न द्वेष्टि (does not hate) + न अनुषज्जते (does not get attached) + सत्त्वसमाविष्टः (saturated with sattva) + मेधावी (intelligent) + छिन्नसंशयः (whose doubts are cut). Two negated finite verbs frame the equanimity; the three nominative adjectives name the inner condition that produces it.

18.10.title: अकुशले / कुशले — locatives of indifference
18.10.body: Both are सप्तमी एकवचन — "in unpleasant action," "in pleasant action." The त्यागी is locative-equanimous: regardless of which kind of action lands in front of him, the inner stance does not tilt. This is the practical sign of छिन्नसंशयः.

18.11.title: देहभृता — the embodied cannot drop action entirely
18.11.body: देहभृता is तृतीया एकवचन ("by the body-bearer") — instrumental of the agent under passive शक्यम्. अशेषतः ("without remainder") is the adverb that matters: total renunciation of action is grammatically impossible for the embodied. So Krishna redefines renunciation as renunciation of फल.

18.11.title: त्यागी इति अभिधीयते — the true definition
18.11.body: अभिधीयते is passive लट् of अभि + √धा ("is called"). The कर्मफलत्यागी is the only one who deserves the title त्यागी. Krishna here completes the inversion begun in 2.47-48 and 5.10-12: tyāga is not about ceasing to act but about ceasing to grasp.

18.12.title: त्रिविधं कर्मफलम् — desired, undesired, mixed
18.12.body: अनिष्टम्, इष्टम्, मिश्रम् — three नपुंसक adjectives modifying फलम्. प्रेत्य is the absolutive of प्र + √इ ("having died/gone beyond"). The fruit lands on the अत्यागिनाम् after death; the संन्यासी, by releasing फल, escapes this post-mortem accounting entirely.

18.12.title: क्वचित् — never, anywhere
18.12.body: न क्वचित् — "not anywhere, not ever." The सम्बन्ध (correlation) is sharp: अत्यागिनाम् फलम् भवति; संन्यासिनाम् न क्वचित्. The verse closes the tyāga section by stating the metaphysical consequence: karmaphala has no purchase on the one who has released फल.

18.13.title: पञ्चैतानि — five causes of every action
18.13.body: पञ्च एतानि कारणानि (नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन) is the rhetorical setup. साङ्ख्ये कृतान्ते is सप्तमी — "in the Sāṃkhya that concludes [analysis]." प्रोक्तानि (past passive participle, agreeing with कारणानि): "have been declared." निबोध (लोट्, मध्यम पुरुष) — "learn well."

18.13.title: सिद्धये — चतुर्थी of purpose
18.13.body: सिद्धये is चतुर्थी एकवचन ("for the accomplishment of"), governing सर्वकर्मणाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन). The five causes are not optional contributors — they are the necessary conditions for any कर्म to come to fruition. The 16-17 conclusion (the one who sees the आत्मा alone as agent does not see) presupposes this enumeration.

18.14.title: The five enumerated — body, agent, organs, efforts, daiva
18.14.body: अधिष्ठानम् (the seat — the body), कर्ता (the ego-agent), करणम् पृथग्विधम् (various sense-instruments), विविधाश्च पृथक्चेष्टाः (multiple distinct efforts/pulses of vital activity), and दैवम् — divine causation — as the fifth (पञ्चमम्). The list moves from gross to subtle to transcendent.

18.14.title: दैवम् — the unsettling fifth
18.14.body: दैवम् (नपुंसक प्रथमा) is the unseen factor — the totality of past karma, divine ordering, the result-conferring power. Sāṃkhya naming the दैवम् alongside the agent is striking: even the dualist enumeration acknowledges that something beyond the visible four shapes the outcome.

18.15.title: शरीरवाङ्मनोभिः — instrumental triad
18.15.body: शरीर-वाक्-मनस् in तृतीया बहुवचन: "by body, speech, mind." These are the three channels (kāyika, vācika, mānasika) through which any कर्म is initiated. प्रारभते (आत्मनेपद लट् of प्र + √रभ्) — "undertakes for himself."

18.15.title: न्याय्यं वा विपरीतं वा — moral, immoral, same five causes
18.15.body: Whether the act is righteous or its opposite, पञ्च एते हेतवः apply equally. The five-cause analysis is morally neutral metaphysics — it is structural, not evaluative. The moral evaluation comes from the doer's intention and the act's conformity to dharma, not from the causal scaffold.

18.16.title: केवलं कर्तारम् — the fatal singular
18.16.body: केवलम् ("alone, solely") is the diagnostic word. Anyone who sees the आत्मा as केवलं कर्तारम् — sole agent, ignoring the other four causes — is दुर्मतिः. अकृतबुद्धित्वात् (पञ्चमी, abstract noun in -त्व): "because of the unrefined-intellect-ness."

18.16.title: न स पश्यति — the double sight
18.16.body: पश्यति ("sees") is repeated with negation: he "sees" (i.e., thinks he sees) but does not see. Krishna plays on the verb: cognitive seeing without metaphysical seeing is non-seeing. The grammatical repetition makes the verdict cut sharper than a flat denial would.

18.17.title: नाहंकृतो भावः — the agent-less agent
18.17.body: यस्य नाहंकृतो भावः — "whose disposition is not appropriated by the ego." बुद्धिः न लिप्यते — "the intellect is not smeared." The two यस्य clauses (षष्ठी relative) describe the same person. हत्वा (absolutive of √हन्) sets up the radical claim that follows.

18.17.title: हत्वापि न हन्ति न निबध्यते — Arjuna's resolution
18.17.body: "Even having slain these worlds, he does not slay, is not bound." इमान् लोकान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन) is the object. The verse delivers the answer to the entire 1.28-2.9 paralysis: the act of killing, performed by one without ahaṃkartṛ-bhāva, does not bind. This is the closing reaffirmation of 2.19-21.

18.18.title: Threefold कर्मचोदना, threefold कर्मसङ्ग्रह
18.18.body: ज्ञानम्, ज्ञेयम्, परिज्ञाता — three nominatives forming the कर्मचोदना (impulse to action): the knowledge, the knowable, the knower. करणम्, कर्म, कर्ता — the instrument, the act, the agent — form the कर्मसङ्ग्रह (collection in which action consists). Two triads, six analytical handles.

18.18.title: इति — the labeling particle that organizes
18.18.body: इति appears twice (after परिज्ञाता and after कर्ता), each time closing one triad. The verse is grammatically a definition by enumeration: each triad is labeled and offered as the framework for the gunatraya analysis of 18.19-39.

18.19.title: Three things × three guṇas = the analytical grid
18.19.body: ज्ञानम्, कर्म, कर्ता are the three targets; गुणभेदतः (तसिल् suffix, ablatival sense: "by guṇa-distinction") is the principle. प्रोच्यते (passive लट् of प्र + √वच्) — "is declared." गुणसङ्ख्याने सप्तमी — "in the Sāṃkhya analysis."

18.19.title: यथावत् — exactly as it is
18.19.body: यथावत् is the adverb "in correspondence with reality." शृणु तानि अपि (लोट् of √श्रु) — "listen to them too." The अपि acknowledges that Krishna has already begun classifying tyāga; now ज्ञान-कर्म-कर्तृ enter the same triadic frame.

18.20.title: एकं अव्ययम् अविभक्तम् — the sāttvika sight
18.20.body: Three accusatives modifying भावम्: "one, imperishable, undivided." सर्वभूतेषु (सप्तमी), विभक्तेषु (सप्तमी, agreeing with भूतेषु) — "in all beings, in the divided ones." The sāttvika ज्ञान sees the undivided in the divided. ईक्षते is आत्मनेपद लट् of √ईक्ष्.

18.20.title: विद्धि — imperative of recognition
18.20.body: विद्धि is लोट् of √विद् (मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन) — "know." Krishna does not say "this is sāttvika" descriptively; he commands Arjuna to recognize it. The verse is structurally identical to 18.21-22 (एकं → पृथक्त्वेन → कृत्स्नवत्-एकस्मिन्), letting the contrast register.

18.21.title: पृथक्त्वेन — the rājasa epistemology
18.21.body: पृथक्त्वेन is तृतीया एकवचन ("by way of separateness") — the instrumental mode of cognition. नानाभावान् पृथग्विधान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन) — "manifold dispositions of various kinds." The rājasa knows the beings as plural, distinct, never traced back to the एक भाव.

18.21.title: वेत्ति — the verb of empirical knowing
18.21.body: वेत्ति (लट् of √विद्, परस्मैपद) is third-person singular: "he knows." Sāttvika ज्ञान used ईक्षते (sees as a totality); rājasa ज्ञान uses वेत्ति (knows discretely). The lexical choice tracks the cognitive mode: vision versus enumeration.

18.22.title: Four damning adjectives for tāmasa ज्ञान
18.22.body: कृत्स्नवत् (as if it were the whole), एकस्मिन् कार्ये सक्तम् (clinging to one effect), अहैतुकम् (without reason), अतत्त्वार्थवत् (without grasp of reality), अल्पम् (paltry). Each compound or adverb compounds the verdict — tāmasa knowledge mistakes a fragment for the totality.

18.22.title: उदाहृतम् — the verdict participle
18.22.body: उदाहृतम् (past passive participle of उद् + आ + √हृ, "is cited / is declared as an example") closes 18.22, 18.25 (तत्तामसमुच्यते uses उच्यते; उदाहृतम् instead closes 18.24 rājasa karma), and 18.39. The pattern: detailed description, then judicial verdict by passive participle (उदाहृतम् / उच्यते / परिकीर्तितः) — Krishna varies the closing verb-form across the triads.

18.23.title: Four conditions of sāttvika कर्म
18.23.body: नियतम् (prescribed), सङ्गरहितम् (free of attachment), अरागद्वेषतः कृतम् (done without attraction or aversion), अफलप्रेप्सुना (तृतीया, "by one not craving fruit"). Note अफलप्रेप्सुना is the agent in instrumental under passive कृतम्. Each adjective negates one defect that 18.24-25 will showcase.

18.23.title: अरागद्वेषतः — the ablative-adverbial form
18.23.body: अराग-द्वेषतः is the तसिल् suffix on a dvandva compound: "from the position of non-attachment-and-non-aversion." The form names the inner stance from which the action issues. This is the operational definition of sāttvika action that 18.26 will personify in the कर्ता.

18.24.title: कामेप्सुना साहंकारेण — instrumental agents of rājasa
18.24.body: कामेप्सुना (तृतीया, "by one desiring objects") and साहंकारेण (तृतीया, "or by one full of ego") are both instruments of the passive क्रियते. वा पुनः marks the alternation. The repeated -ना/-णा endings rhetorically hammer the two motives of rājasa action.

18.24.title: बहुलायासम् — much labor for an unstable end
18.24.body: बहुलायासम् ("with abundant exertion") is the diagnostic adjective. Rājasa action is not lazy — it is frantic. The verse pairs an inflamed motive (कामेप्सु / साहंकार) with disproportionate effort, contrasting with the calm नियतम् of 18.23.

18.25.title: Four things the tāmasa agent disregards
18.25.body: अनुबन्धम् (consequence), क्षयम् (loss), हिंसाम् (violence), पौरुषम् (one's own capacity) — all द्वितीया, all objects of अनपेक्ष्य (absolutive: "without regarding"). The agent doesn't weigh outcome, attrition, harm done, or strength available. मोहात् (पञ्चमी) is the trigger.

18.25.title: आरभ्यते — passive of the indifferent launch
18.25.body: आरभ्यते is passive लट् of आ + √रभ्. The action is not framed as agent-driven but as something that simply gets begun under tāmasa conditions. The grammar mirrors the content: agency itself is fogged over.

18.26.title: Four markers of the sāttvika कर्ता
18.26.body: मुक्तसङ्गः (free from attachment), अनहंवादी (not ego-asserting), धृत्युत्साहसमन्वितः (endowed with steadfastness and enthusiasm), सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः निर्विकारः (unchanged in success or failure). The compounds stack rapidly — each names a virtue that 18.27 / 18.28 will negatively mirror.

18.26.title: सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः — locative dvandva of equanimity
18.26.body: सिद्धि-असिद्ध्योः is सप्तमी द्विवचन (locative dual) of a dvandva compound: "in success and failure." The dual form is precise — there are exactly two cases to be equanimous about, and निर्विकारः (unchanged) reports the agent's stance across both.

18.27.title: Six nominatives painting the rājasa कर्ता
18.27.body: रागी, कर्मफलप्रेप्सुः, लुब्धः, हिंसात्मकः, अशुचिः, हर्षशोकान्वितः — six adjectives in प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with कर्ता. Each names a passion-driven trait: desire for the object, greed, violent disposition, impurity, swing between joy and sorrow. The rājasa agent is volatile.

18.27.title: हर्षशोकान्वितः — the diagnostic oscillation
18.27.body: हर्ष-शोक-अन्वितः is a tatpuruṣa-bahuvrīhi: "accompanied by joy and grief." Where sāttvika is निर्विकारः, rājasa swings between हर्ष and शोक as फल lands or fails. The structural contrast with 18.26 is exact: one is a constant, the other a pendulum.

18.28.title: Eight epithets of the tāmasa agent
18.28.body: अयुक्तः (unfit/undisciplined), प्राकृतः (vulgar/coarse), स्तब्धः (rigid), शठः (deceitful), नैष्कृतिकः (one who undoes others' work), अलसः (lazy), विषादी (despondent), दीर्घसूत्री (procrastinator). The list goes from disposition to action: each fault compounds with the others.

18.28.title: दीर्घसूत्री — the long-thread procrastinator
18.28.body: दीर्घसूत्री is literally "long-threaded" (suffix -इन्) — one who draws out tasks endlessly. This last epithet is the practical signature: tāmasa is the agent whose work is never finished because it is never properly begun. Contrast 18.26's धृत्युत्साहसमन्वितः.

18.29.title: Buddhi and dhṛti — two more triads incoming
18.29.body: बुद्धेः and धृतेः are षष्ठी एकवचन — "of the intellect, and also of steadfastness." गुणतः (तसिल्) — "by way of guṇa." त्रिविधम् (three-fold), अशेषेण (without remainder), पृथक्त्वेन (distinctly) stack adverbially around प्रोच्यमानम् — "being explained."

18.29.title: धनञ्जय — the wealth-winning vocative
18.29.body: धनञ्जय ("conqueror of wealth") frames Arjuna as one fit to receive distinctions. Krishna stages each triad-cluster with a vocative — here, signaling that the bauddha + dhārya analysis is itself a kind of wealth to be won by precise distinction.

18.30.title: Four pairs the sāttvika buddhi distinguishes
18.30.body: प्रवृत्ति-निवृत्ति (engagement/disengagement), कार्य-अकार्य (to-be-done/not-to-be-done), भय-अभय (fear/fearlessness), बन्ध-मोक्ष (bondage/liberation) — all in द्वितीया as objects of वेत्ति. The sāttvika buddhi is precisely the cognitive faculty that draws these decisive dharmic distinctions.

18.30.title: वेत्ति — third-person singular of pure cognition
18.30.body: वेत्ति (लट् of √विद्) is the verb of discriminating knowledge. The sāttvika buddhi is defined not by its content but by what it can correctly distinguish — what to take up, what to drop, what to fear, what binds, what frees. The list runs from practical action to ultimate liberation.

18.31.title: अयथावत् — the rājasa cognitive flaw
18.31.body: अयथावत् ("not in conformity with reality") is the adverbial verdict. Rājasa buddhi attempts the same distinctions sāttvika buddhi makes — धर्म/अधर्म, कार्य/अकार्य — but distorts them. प्रजानाति ("knows distinctly") is undercut by its modifier: the prefix प्र- is intact, the cognition is not.

18.31.title: Same vocabulary, opposite verdict
18.31.body: Verse 30 and 31 share the same objects (धर्म, अधर्म, कार्य, अकार्य) — only the adverb differs. यथावत् vs अयथावत्. The grammar drives home that rājasa is not lack of interest in distinctions but flawed execution of them; the buddhi engages but garbles.

18.32.title: तमसा आवृता — the wrapped intellect
18.32.body: तमसा is तृतीया एकवचन ("by darkness"), आवृता (कृदन्त, प्रथमा स्त्रीलिङ्ग, agreeing with बुद्धिः) — "covered." The tāmasa buddhi is not merely flawed; it is wrapped. The instrumental case marks the active agent of the covering — tamas itself.

18.32.title: अधर्मं धर्मम् इति मन्यते — total inversion
18.32.body: मन्यते (आत्मनेपद लट् of √मन्) — "reckons." The तामसी buddhi takes अधर्म to be धर्म and (as the verse adds) sees all things विपरीतान् — inverted. Where rājasa is distortion (31), tāmasa is total inversion. Three buddhis, three degrees of distance from reality.

18.33.title: योगेन अव्यभिचारिण्या — the unwavering dhṛti
18.33.body: अव्यभिचारिण्या is तृतीया एकवचन स्त्रीलिङ्ग (agreeing with धृत्या), from अ-वि-अभि-√चर् with -इन्: "non-deviating." योगेन — by means of yoga. The sāttvika dhṛti holds the mind-prāṇa-indriya activities in steady alignment because it is fused with the yogic discipline.

18.33.title: मनःप्राणेन्द्रियक्रियाः — the triple field of steadiness
18.33.body: मनस्, प्राण, इन्द्रिय — three regions whose activities (क्रियाः, द्वितीया बहुवचन स्त्रीलिङ्ग) the dhṛti holds in place. धारयते (आत्मनेपद लट् of √धृ): "holds, sustains." Sāttvika dhṛti is the faculty that keeps the inner instruments aligned across long time.

18.34.title: धर्म-काम-अर्थान् — the trivarga grip
18.34.body: धर्म-काम-अर्थ are द्वितीया बहुवचन — the three worldly puruṣārthas (mokṣa is excluded). The rājasa dhṛti holds these three steadily, but with प्रसङ्गेन ("with clinging") and as a फलाकाङ्क्षी ("fruit-craver," प्रथमा एकवचन, agreeing with the implied agent). Steadiness, yes — but for outcomes.

18.34.title: प्रसङ्गेन — the rājasa modifier
18.34.body: प्रसङ्गेन is तृतीया एकवचन — "by way of attachment / clinging association." The sāttvika dhṛti was योगेन; the rājasa is प्रसङ्गेन. Same dhṛti-act, opposite inner posture: one bonds through yoga, the other through clinging to the trivarga.

18.35.title: Five states the tāmasa dhṛti refuses to release
18.35.body: स्वप्नम् (dreaminess/lethargy), भयम् (fear), शोकम् (grief), विषादम् (despondency), मदम् (intoxication) — five द्वितीया objects of न विमुञ्चति ("does not release"). The dhṛti is steadfast in the wrong direction — clutching dysfunction instead of dispelling it.

18.35.title: दुर्मेधा — the agent of misdirected steadiness
18.35.body: दुर्मेधाः (प्रथमा एकवचन, "dull-witted") is the implied subject of विमुञ्चति. The tāmasa dhṛti is a function of दुर्मेधा — it persists because the discrimination needed to release the five states is itself absent. Bad steadiness on a bad foundation.

18.36.title: Sukha's threefold announcement
18.36.body: सुखम् त्विदानीम् त्रिविधम् — "now, threefold pleasure." Krishna announces the last triad with the temporal इदानीम् ("now"). अभ्यासात् (पञ्चमी, "from repeated practice") is the diagnostic of the sāttvika sukha that 18.37 will detail.

18.36.title: दुःखान्तं निगच्छति — the pleasure that ends suffering
18.36.body: निगच्छति ("goes to, attains") takes the द्वितीया दुःखान्तम् — "the end of suffering." The sāttvika sukha's diagnostic is teleological: it leads, through repetition, to the cessation of duḥkha. Note the contrast with rājasa sukha (38), which begins as amṛta and ends as विष.

18.37.title: विषमिव → अमृतोपमम् — the sāttvika reversal
18.37.body: अग्रे (locative: "at the start") विषम् इव ("like poison"); परिणामे (locative: "at maturation") अमृत-उपमम् ("like nectar"). The sāttvika sukha inverts the rājasa trajectory of 18.38. Sādhanā tastes bitter; its fruit is amṛta.

18.37.title: आत्मबुद्धिप्रसादजम् — the source clause
18.37.body: आत्म-बुद्धि-प्रसाद-जम् is a long तत्पुरुष: "born from the clarity/grace of the self-intellect." The suffix -ज ("born of") names the origin. Sāttvika sukha is not external — it issues from the buddhi's settled luminosity, which is why it endures.

18.38.title: विषयेन्द्रियसंयोगात् — the ablative of contact
18.38.body: विषय-इन्द्रिय-संयोगात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन): "from the conjunction of object and sense." The rājasa sukha is born at the point of contact — momentary, conditioned. The grammar names its dependency: sense + object meet, and pleasure flares.

18.38.title: Reversed trajectory — अमृत → विष
18.38.body: अग्रे अमृतोपमम्, परिणामे विषमिव — "at the start, like nectar; at maturation, like poison." The mirror-image of 18.37. Same lexical pair (अमृत/विष) in opposite locative slots. Krishna's grammar makes the contrast surgical: timing inverts the verdict.

18.39.title: मोहनम् आत्मनः — pleasure that confuses the self
18.39.body: मोहनम् (कृदन्त: "deluding") agrees with सुखम्. आत्मनः is षष्ठी ("of the self") or चतुर्थी ("for the self"): the tāmasa sukha is delusion-producing for the very self that takes it. यदग्रे चानुबन्धे च — "both at the start and in the consequence."

18.39.title: निद्रा-आलस्य-प्रमाद-उत्थम् — the tāmasa source
18.39.body: Sleep, laziness, heedlessness — the three sources from which the tāmasa sukha rises (उत्थम्, from उद् + √स्था). The compound names the dysfunction directly. Where sāttvika sukha is born of आत्मबुद्धिप्रसाद, tāmasa is born of stupor.

18.40.title: The universal claim — no being escapes the guṇas
18.40.body: न तत् अस्ति पृथिव्याम् वा दिवि देवेषु वा पुनः — "there is nothing on earth, or in heaven among the gods." Three locatives sweep the domains. एभिः त्रिभिः गुणैः मुक्तम् (तृतीया) — "freed from these three guṇas." The guṇa-triad is the universal coordinate system.

18.40.title: सत्त्वम् प्रकृतिजैः — pun and seal
18.40.body: सत्त्वम् here means "being / existent thing" (not the guṇa sattva); प्रकृतिजैः (तृतीया बहुवचन, "by those born of prakṛti") closes the gunatraya analysis. Every existent is woven by these three. The analysis that began in 18.19 now declares its universal scope.

18.41.title: स्वभावप्रभवैः गुणैः — varṇa from nature, not birth-alone
18.41.body: ब्राह्मण-क्षत्रिय-विशाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन of the dvandva), शूद्राणाम् च — four varṇas. Their कर्माणि are प्रविभक्तानि (past passive participle: "distributed") स्वभावप्रभवैः गुणैः (तृतीया बहुवचन: "by guṇas born of one's own nature"). The classification is causal — guṇa determines fitness.

18.41.title: परन्तप — the scorching vocative
18.41.body: परन्तप ("scorcher of foes") is the सम्बोधन — fitting before the kṣātra description of 18.43. Krishna prepares Arjuna to hear his own varṇa-dharma announced in the third verse of the series, by addressing him in his warrior aspect first.

18.42.title: Nine inner qualities of brāhmaṇa-karma
18.42.body: शम (mind-control), दम (sense-control), तप, शौच, क्षान्ति, आर्जव, ज्ञान, विज्ञान, आस्तिक्य — nine प्रथमा एकवचन nouns, predicated as ब्रह्मकर्म स्वभावजम् ("the brahmin work, born of nature"). Note: all nine are inner disciplines, none transactional.

18.42.title: ज्ञानं विज्ञानम् — the cognition pair
18.42.body: ज्ञानम् (scriptural/conceptual knowledge) and विज्ञानम् (realized/discriminative knowing) appear as a pair. The brāhmaṇa's karma is not action in the world's usual sense — it is the cultivation of states that make the seeing of ज्ञान/विज्ञान possible.

18.43.title: Seven martial qualities of the kṣatriya
18.43.body: शौर्य (valor), तेज (radiance/intensity), धृति (steadfastness), दाक्ष्य (skill), युद्धे च अपलायनम् (non-flight in battle), दान (giving), ईश्वरभाव (lordly disposition) — seven nominatives forming the natural kṣātra-karma. युद्धे is सप्तमी, situating अपलायनम् precisely.

18.43.title: अपलायनम् — the un-flight
18.43.body: अपलायनम् is the negation of पलायन (flight) — formed with the अ- privative on the action noun. The grammar makes this a positive virtue, not just absence of cowardice. For Arjuna, who in 1.45-46 contemplated abandoning the battle, this single word is a direct address.

18.44.title: Three trades and the service-work
18.44.body: कृषि-गौरक्ष्य-वाणिज्यम् — three nominative-singular fields in dvandva (agriculture, cattle-protection, trade) constituting वैश्यकर्म स्वभावजम्. परिचर्या-आत्मकम् कर्म — "work whose essence is service" — is the शूद्र's स्वभावज contribution.

18.44.title: अपि — even/also for śūdra
18.44.body: शूद्रस्य अपि — "of the śūdra too." The अपि includes the fourth varṇa within the same स्वभावज framework. Each varṇa's work is born of nature; none is excluded from the principle. The next verse (18.45) will make this universal: each finds sidddhi through his own work.

18.45.title: स्वे स्वे कर्मणि — locative of one's own work
18.45.body: स्वे स्वे (सप्तमी एकवचन, reduplicated to mean "each in his own") कर्मणि अभिरतः ("delighting"). The reduplication distributes: each varṇa, each individual, in his own assigned field. संसिद्धिम् लभते — "attains perfect accomplishment."

18.45.title: तत् शृणु — the pivot to 18.46
18.45.body: यथा विन्दति तत् शृणु — "hear how he finds it." The verse explicitly sets up the next: 18.45 declares the principle; 18.46 will give the method (worshipping the all-pervading source through one's work). The grammar marks the transition with the relative यथा / correlative तत्.

18.46.title: यतः-येन — the double relative
18.46.body: यतः प्रवृत्तिः भूतानाम् ("from whom is the issuance of beings"), येन सर्वम् इदम् ततम् ("by whom all this is pervaded"). Two relative clauses identifying the same divine reality. ततम् is the past passive participle of √तन् ("stretched, pervaded").

18.46.title: स्वकर्मणा अभ्यर्च्य — worship through one's own work
18.46.body: स्वकर्मणा is तृतीया एकवचन — "by means of one's own work." अभ्यर्च्य is the absolutive ("having worshipped"). The radical thesis: one's allotted varṇa-karma, performed as worship of the all-pervading source, is itself the path to सिद्धि. No separate ritual required.

18.47.title: श्रेयान् स्वधर्मः विगुणः — the famous declaration repeated
18.47.body: श्रेयान् (comparative of श्रेयस्) — "better." स्वधर्मः विगुणः ("one's own dharma, though imperfectly done") is preferred over परधर्मात् स्वनुष्ठितात् (पञ्चमी of comparison, "than another's dharma well-performed"). This is the 3.35 dictum, restated as the chapter closes.

18.47.title: किल्बिषम् न आप्नोति — no sin clings
18.47.body: स्वभावनियतम् कर्म कुर्वन् ("doing the work fixed by his own nature") — कुर्वन् is शतृ-pratyaya (present active participle). किल्बिषम् न आप्नोति — "does not incur sin." The natural-fit work, even with technical defect, does not produce karmic stain — because the agent is operating within his own grain.

18.48.title: सहजम् सदोषम् अपि — the inborn, even if flawed
18.48.body: सहजम् कर्म ("work born along with one") सदोषम् अपि ("even if defective") — न त्यजेत् (विधिलिङ्, "should not abandon"). The injunction protects the principle of 18.47 against the temptation to flee one's nature when it appears imperfect.

18.48.title: धूमेन अग्निः इव आवृताः — the smoke-fire simile
18.48.body: सर्वारम्भाः ("all undertakings") दोषेण आवृताः — "are wrapped by defect," धूमेन अग्निः इव ("as fire by smoke"). Two तृतीया instrumentals (दोषेण, धूमेन) anchor the simile. No karma in saṃsāra is fault-free; the test is fit, not purity.

18.49.title: Three nominatives of the renunciate's inner state
18.49.body: असक्तबुद्धिः (whose buddhi is unattached), जितात्मा (whose self is conquered), विगतस्पृहः (whose longing has departed) — three प्रथमा एकवचन bahuvrīhi compounds describing the agent. नैष्कर्म्यसिद्धिम् परमाम् — "the supreme accomplishment of action-lessness."

18.49.title: संन्यासेन अधिगच्छति — the instrumental of attainment
18.49.body: संन्यासेन is तृतीया एकवचन ("by means of saṃnyāsa"). The naiṣkarmya-siddhi is not attained by ceasing to act but by the inner saṃnyāsa of attachment, ego, longing. This restates 18.11's definition of the true त्यागी on the level of supreme attainment.

18.50.title: ब्रह्म-attainment — समासेन एव
18.50.body: सिद्धिम् प्राप्तः ("having attained siddhi") यथा ब्रह्म तथा आप्नोति — "as he attains brahman, so [hear]." समासेन ("in brief") एव is Krishna's pedagogical promise: the next six verses (51-56) will compress the entire jñāna-niṣṭhā path. Whatever was developed in chapters 5-6 will be summarized.

18.50.title: निष्ठा ज्ञानस्य या परा — the supreme establishment
18.50.body: निष्ठा ("firm establishment") ज्ञानस्य (षष्ठी) — "of knowledge." या परा — "which is supreme." This is the technical term for the consummation of the jñāna-yoga path; 18.51-55 unfolds its conditions in a single long descriptive sweep.

18.51.title: बुद्ध्या विशुद्धया युक्तः — instrumental of purity
18.51.body: बुद्ध्या विशुद्धया (तृतीया एकवचन स्त्रीलिङ्ग, "with purified intellect") युक्तः ("yoked, equipped"). The opening condition: a buddhi rid of rājasic-tāmasic admixture, of the sort 18.30 described.

18.51.title: Three absolutives setting the discipline
18.51.body: नियम्य (having restrained — आत्मानम्; ल्यबन्त, नि + √यम् + ल्यप्), त्यक्त्वा (having abandoned — विषयान्; क्त्वान्त, prefix-less √त्यज् + क्त्वा), व्युदस्य (having cast away — रागद्वेषौ; ल्यबन्त, वि + उद् + √अस् + ल्यप्). Pāṇinian rule: ल्यप् replaces क्त्वा when a prefix is present (समासेऽनञ्पूर्वे क्त्वो ल्यप्). Note रागद्वेषौ is द्विवचन — the pair, treated as a single complex.

18.52.title: External regimen for the jñāna-niṣṭhā
18.52.body: विविक्तसेवी (frequenter of solitude), लघ्वाशी (light-eater), यतवाक्कायमानसः (one whose speech, body, and mind are controlled) — three प्रथमा compounds, plus ध्यानयोगपरः (devoted to dhyāna-yoga), नित्यम्, वैराग्यम् समुपाश्रितः (firmly resorting to dispassion).

18.52.title: Five disciplines, one direction
18.52.body: The verse stacks lifestyle prescriptions without a finite verb — the sentence will only close with 18.53's कल्पते. Each item (solitude, restraint, dhyāna, vairāgya) addresses one channel through which the mind disperses. The grammar's accumulation mirrors the gathered, contained life it describes.

18.53.title: Six things to release before brahma-bhāva
18.53.body: अहंकारम्, बलम्, दर्पम्, कामम्, क्रोधम्, परिग्रहम् — six द्वितीया objects of विमुच्य (absolutive: "having released"). The list moves from the metaphysical (ego, force, pride) to the psychological (desire, anger) to the material (acquisition). Each is a hand still gripping.

18.53.title: ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते — fitness for becoming-brahman
18.53.body: ब्रह्मभूयाय is चतुर्थी एकवचन (purpose: "for the state of being-brahman"). कल्पते (आत्मनेपद लट् of √कल्प्) — "becomes fit / is competent." The grammar names a transformation: not attainment as gain, but fitness as readiness. The verse closes the long sentence begun in 18.51.

18.54.title: ब्रह्मभूतः — the threshold crossed
18.54.body: ब्रह्मभूतः (past passive participle: "having become brahman") is the new state. प्रसन्नात्मा (calmed-souled), न शोचति, न काङ्क्षति, समः सर्वेषु भूतेषु — four markers of the brahma-state. The verse echoes 2.55-72's sthitaprajña.

18.54.title: मद्भक्तिं लभते पराम् — the surprising turn
18.54.body: After ब्रह्मभूत, Krishna does not stop. The brahmabhūta attains मद्भक्तिम् पराम् ("my supreme bhakti") — द्वितीया. This is the chapter's pivotal claim: jñāna-niṣṭhā does not terminate at brahma-realization but blossoms into para-bhakti. The Advaita-Bhakti junction lives in this verse.

18.55.title: भक्त्या — instrumental of recognition
18.55.body: भक्त्या (तृतीया एकवचन) माम् अभिजानाति यावान् यः च अस्मि तत्त्वतः — "by bhakti one knows me, how much and what, in truth." The relative-pair यावान्...यः maps both the extent (यावान् = "how great") and the identity (यः = "who") of the divine; both are relatives, picked up by an implicit तावान्...सः.

18.55.title: विशते तदनन्तरम् — entry, not addition
18.55.body: विशते (आत्मनेपद लट् of √विश्) — "enters." तदनन्तरम् ("immediately thereafter") — once tattvataḥ jñāna is achieved through bhakti, entry follows without remainder. This is the climax of the jñāna-niṣṭhā sequence: knowing → entering, not knowing → leaving.

18.56.title: मद्व्यपाश्रयः — the bahuvrīhi shield
18.56.body: सर्वकर्माणि कुर्वाणः (शानच्, present middle participle: "performing all actions") मद्व्यपाश्रयः ("having me as refuge"). The action continues; only the inner refuge has shifted. मत्प्रसादात् (पञ्चमी) — "by my grace" — names the source of the शाश्वत पदम्.

18.56.title: शाश्वतं पदम् अव्ययम् — the imperishable goal
18.56.body: शाश्वतम् (eternal), अव्ययम् (imperishable) modify पदम् ("station, abode"). All three accusatives are objects of अवाप्नोति. The state attained through me-refuge while still acting is itself the para-pada — work is not an obstacle for the one rightly oriented.

18.57.title: मयि संन्यस्य — locative of surrender
18.57.body: चेतसा (तृतीया, "with the mind") सर्वकर्माणि मयि (सप्तमी, "in me") संन्यस्य (absolutive of सम् + नि + √अस्: "having laid down"). The locative मयि is the destination of the depositing. This is the operative formula of karma-saṃnyāsa as taught here.

18.57.title: मच्चित्तः सततं भव — imperative of constant orientation
18.57.body: मच्चित्तः (bahuvrīhi: "whose mind is on me") सततम् (always) भव (लोट् of √भू, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन): "be." The verse stacks three mat- compounds: मत्परः, बुद्धियोगम् उपाश्रित्य, मच्चित्तः — three angles of the same inner alignment.

18.58.title: मत्प्रसादात् तरिष्यसि — the future of crossing
18.58.body: तरिष्यसि (लृट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन — note the future infix -ष्य-) — "you shall cross." सर्वदुर्गाणि (द्वितीया बहुवचन) — "all difficult passes." मत्प्रसादात् (पञ्चमी) names the means. The grammar promises crossing, not painlessness.

18.58.title: विनङ्क्ष्यसि — the contingent ruin
18.58.body: अथ चेत् त्वम् अहंकारात् न श्रोष्यसि ("if, from ego, you will not listen") — विनङ्क्ष्यसि (लृट् of वि + √नश्, "you will perish"). Krishna pairs the salvific future with its alternative. The two -ष्यसि forms — तरिष्यसि / विनङ्क्ष्यसि — are the chapter's sharpest fork in the road.

18.59.title: अहंकारम् आश्रित्य — taking refuge in ego
18.59.body: अहंकारम् आश्रित्य (absolutive: "having resorted to the ego") — the opposite of मद्व्यपाश्रय. न योत्स्ये (लृट् आत्मनेपद, "I shall not fight") is what Arjuna imagines saying — Krishna quotes the resolve only to dismiss it as मिथ्या (false).

18.59.title: प्रकृतिः त्वाम् नियोक्ष्यति — nature will employ you
18.59.body: नियोक्ष्यति (लृट् of नि + √युज्) — "will yoke, will employ." The 3rd-person future has प्रकृति as subject, त्वाम् as object. The ego thinks it can opt out; nature itself, the स्वभाव-encoded kṣatriya prakṛti, will compel the body to act regardless.

18.60.title: करिष्यसि अवशः — you will do, even unwillingly
18.60.body: स्वभावजेन कर्मणा निबद्धः ("bound by the action born of your own nature") — कर्तुम् न इच्छसि यत् मोहात् ("what, out of delusion, you do not wish to do") — करिष्यसि अवशः अपि ("you will do, even helplessly"). The grammar pins the agency to nature, not to the wishing-mind.

18.60.title: अवशः अपि — the involuntary clause
18.60.body: अवशः ("without choice") is the diagnostic adjective. अपि intensifies: even involuntarily. Krishna is closing Arjuna's escape routes one by one — the action will happen; only the inner orientation is still up for choice. This sets up the antarātmā teaching of 18.61.

18.61.title: हृद्देशे — locative of the inner driver
18.61.body: ईश्वरः सर्वभूतानाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन) हृद्देशे (सप्तमी, "in the region of the heart") तिष्ठति. The Lord stands in the heart-region of all beings. भ्रामयन् (शतृ-pratyaya: "causing to revolve") names the action: he rotates all beings.

18.61.title: यन्त्रारूढानि मायया — the machinery metaphor
18.61.body: यन्त्र-आरूढानि (past passive participle: "mounted on a machine") modifies सर्वभूतानि. मायया (तृतीया) — "by māyā." Living beings are mounted on the cosmic mechanism and rotated by māyā at the antaryāmin's signal. This is the Gītā's strongest assertion of inner-divine causation.

18.62.title: तम् एव शरणं गच्छ — imperative of refuge
18.62.body: तम् (the ईश्वर of 18.61) एव (precisely him) शरणम् गच्छ (लोट्, "go for refuge"). सर्वभावेन (तृतीया, "with all your being") names the totality required. भारत — the vocative situating Arjuna in his lineage as he is told to surrender.

18.62.title: तत्प्रसादात् पराम् शान्तिम् — the result clause
18.62.body: तत्प्रसादात् (पञ्चमी, "by his grace") परां शान्तिम् स्थानं प्राप्स्यसि शाश्वतम् — "you will attain the supreme peace and the eternal abode." Two द्वितीयाs (शान्तिम्, स्थानम्) with the future प्राप्स्यसि. The antarātmā taking refuge in itself, externalized as īśvara, returns the seeker to its source.

18.63.title: गुह्याद् गुह्यतरम् — secret-of-the-secret
18.63.body: गुह्यात् (पञ्चमी of comparison) गुह्यतरम् ("more secret than the secret") — "more hidden than the hidden." आख्यातम् (past passive participle of आ + √ख्या) — "has been declared." Krishna names the chapter's instruction as the deepest layer of esoteric teaching.

18.63.title: यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु — the freedom clause
18.63.body: विमृश्य ("having considered") एतत् अशेषेण ("this, without remainder") — यथा इच्छसि तथा कुरु: "do as you wish." Krishna preserves Arjuna's agency at the very point of revealing the supreme teaching. The teaching does not coerce; it offers itself for विमर्श.

18.64.title: सर्वगुह्यतमम् — the superlative-of-secrets
18.64.body: सर्व-गुह्य-तमम् ("most secret of all") goes beyond 18.63's गुह्यतरम्. भूयः शृणु — "hear again." Krishna escalates the register; what 18.63 announced as the closing, 18.64 reopens with a deeper word still pending.

18.64.title: इष्टः असि मे दृढम् — the affection clause
18.64.body: इष्टः ("beloved") असि (लट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन of √अस्) मे (षष्ठी): "you are dear to me." दृढम् adverbial — "firmly." Krishna grounds the revelation of 18.65 in personal affection, not abstract teaching obligation. The next verse is given because Arjuna is dear.

18.65.title: Four mat-imperatives — the bhakti formula
18.65.body: मन्मनाः भव (have your mind on me), मद्भक्तः (my devotee), मद्याजी (my worshipper), माम् नमस्कुरु (bow to me). Three bahuvrīhi compounds + one imperative verb. The chapter's bhakti-essence packs into four discrete acts.

18.65.title: सत्यं ते प्रतिजाने — Krishna's oath
18.65.body: माम् एव एष्यसि — "you will come to me alone." सत्यम् ते प्रतिजाने (लट् आत्मनेपद of प्रति + √ज्ञा): "I make this promise to you in truth." प्रियः असि मे — the affection-reason restated. The verse offers the formula and underwrites it with a divine oath. The promise is structurally identical to 9.34.

18.66.title: सर्वधर्मान् परित्यज्य — the absolutive that has launched commentaries
18.66.body: सर्वधर्मान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन) is the object of the absolutive परित्यज्य ("having abandoned all dharmas"). The radical accusative scope — सर्व — has produced two millennia of commentarial work: Śaṅkara reading धर्म as ritual prescription, Rāmānuja as ahaṃkārtṛ-bhāva, Madhva as the misperception that any dharma is independent of the Lord. The grammar is precise; the referent is the contested term.

18.66.title: माम् एकम् शरणम् व्रज — the singular refuge
18.66.body: माम् एकम् (द्वितीया, "me alone") शरणम् (predicate accusative, "as refuge") व्रज (लोट् of √व्रज्, "go!"). Then the divine assurance: अहम् त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यः (पञ्चमी बहुवचन, "from all sins") मोक्षयिष्यामि (लृट् of causal √मुच्, "I shall liberate you"). The verse closes with मा शुचः — "do not grieve" — answering 1.46's tears and 2.1's विषाद. The grammar moves: surrender (imperative), divine promise (future), consolation (negative imperative).

18.67.title: Four datives of those unfit to receive
18.67.body: अतपस्काय (one without austerity), अभक्ताय (non-devotee), अशुश्रूषवे (one who does not wish to hear) — three चतुर्थी एकवचन forms naming the unfit recipients. Plus the relative माम् यः अभ्यसूयति ("who is hostile to me"). इदम् वाच्यम् — "this is to be spoken" — gerundive.

18.67.title: न कदाचन — secrecy as protection
18.67.body: न कदाचन ("never, at any time") is the absolute prohibition. The secrecy is not elitism but protection — the unprepared hearer cannot hold the teaching, and the teaching cannot help the hostile. Compare 4.3 (the same logic for the karma-yoga teaching).

18.68.title: मद्भक्तेषु अभिधास्यति — the locative of the worthy audience
18.68.body: मद्भक्तेषु (सप्तमी बहुवचन, "among my devotees") अभिधास्यति (लृट् of अभि + √धा, "will declare") — the one who passes on this secret teaching among Krishna's bhaktas. भक्तिम् मयि (locative) पराम् कृत्वा (absolutive: "having done supreme bhakti to me").

18.68.title: माम् एव एष्यति असंशयः — the unconditional future
18.68.body: एष्यति (लृट् of √इ, "will come") with असंशयः ("without doubt") closes the verse. The transmitter, not just the practitioner, attains Krishna. The grammar of the future tense paired with the modal certainty is striking — Krishna stakes a promise on the act of teaching itself.

18.69.title: प्रियकृत्तमः — superlative of pleasing-action-doing
18.69.body: प्रिय-कृत्-तमः is the तमप् superlative on a compound: "the most pleasing-action-doer." तस्मात् (पञ्चमी, "than him") अन्यः कश्चित् मनुष्येषु (locative) — "any other among men." The verse calls the transmitter of 18.68 the dearest of all to Krishna.

18.69.title: प्रियतरः भुवि — comparative grounded in earth
18.69.body: प्रियतरः (comparative of प्रिय) भुवि (सप्तमी, "on earth"). The double superlative-then-comparative structure stacks the affection. भविता is लुट् (periphrastic future) — "there will be." Even in the future, no one will surpass the transmitter in dearness.

18.70.title: अध्येष्यते — the future of study
18.70.body: अध्येष्यते (लृट् आत्मनेपद of अधि + √इ, "will study for himself") — y इमं धर्म्यम् संवादम् आवयोः (षष्ठी द्विवचन: "this dharmic conversation of the two of us"). The dialogue itself becomes scripture; studying it is a yajña.

18.70.title: ज्ञानयज्ञेन इष्टः स्याम् — sacrifice through knowledge
18.70.body: ज्ञानयज्ञेन (तृतीया) तेन (तृतीया, "by him") अहम् इष्टः (worshipped) स्याम् (विधिलिङ् of √अस्) — इति मे मतिः: "thus is my view." Studying the Gītā-saṃvāda is itself a ज्ञानयज्ञ that worships Krishna — a striking elevation of the text-as-sacrifice.

18.71.title: श्रद्धावान् अनसूयः च — even the listener
18.71.body: श्रद्धावान् ("faith-possessing," -मतुप् suffix) and अनसूयः च ("and non-cavilling") describe the listener. शृणुयात् अपि (विधिलिङ्, optative of √श्रु, "might even hear") — even mere hearing, with right disposition, qualifies.

18.71.title: शुभान् लोकान् — the auspicious worlds of merit-doers
18.71.body: मुक्तः (released from sins) शुभान् लोकान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन) प्राप्नुयात् (विधिलिङ् of √आप्) पुण्यकर्मणाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन, "of those who do meritorious work"). The listener attains the worlds of the meritorious — a substantial promise for so light a contact with the text.

18.72.title: कच्चित् — twice, the gentle interrogative
18.72.body: कच्चित् ("I trust, has it…?") appears twice, framing two questions: "Has this been heard by you with one-pointed mind?" and "Has your delusion-confusion been destroyed?" The doubled कच्चित् is Krishna's tenderness — he asks rather than asserts the success of the teaching.

18.72.title: एकाग्रेण चेतसा — instrumental of one-pointedness
18.72.body: एकाग्रेण चेतसा (तृतीया एकवचन, "by a one-pointed mind") names the required mode of hearing. प्रनष्टः (past passive participle, agreeing with अज्ञानसम्मोहः) — "destroyed." Two vocatives (पार्थ, धनञ्जय) close each line, the teacher's intimate address.

18.73.title: नष्टो मोहः स्मृतिर्लब्धा — Arjuna's clinching reply
18.73.body: Three terse declarations in 8 syllables. नष्टः मोहः ("delusion destroyed"), स्मृतिः लब्धा ("memory regained" — past passive participle of √लभ्, agreeing with स्मृतिः, स्त्रीलिङ्ग), त्वत्प्रसादात् मया अच्युत (पञ्चमी of cause, तृतीया of agent — "by your grace, by me, O Acyuta").

18.73.title: करिष्ये वचनं तव — the resolved future
18.73.body: स्थितः अस्मि ("I stand firm") गतसन्देहः ("with doubt gone") — करिष्ये (लृट् आत्मनेपद of √कृ): "I shall do." वचनम् तव (षष्ठी) — "your word." The whole 1.28-2.9 paralysis resolves in one फ्यूचर tense verb. The grammar marks the choice taken.

18.74.title: Sañjaya resumes — अश्रौषम्
18.74.body: अश्रौषम् is लुङ् (aorist) उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन of √श्रु — "I heard." The augment अ-, sigmatic stem श्रौष्-, and ending -अम् mark the स्-aorist; लङ् of √श्रु would be अशृणवम्. इति अहम् वासुदेवस्य पार्थस्य च महात्मनः (षष्ठी, "of Vāsudeva and the great-souled Pārtha") — the conversation he has heard.

18.74.title: अद्भुतम् रोमहर्षणम् — the marvelous, hair-raising
18.74.body: Two द्वितीया adjectives modify संवादम्: अद्भुतम् (marvelous) and रोमहर्षणम् (causing hair to stand on end). The bahuvrīhi रोम-हर्षण literally means "that which causes the body-hair's erection." Sañjaya's report registers the physical impact of having heard.

18.75.title: व्यासप्रसादात् — the channel of transmission
18.75.body: व्यासप्रसादात् (पञ्चमी, "by Vyāsa's grace") — Sañjaya credits Vyāsa for the divya-cakṣus that enabled his witnessing. श्रुतवान् is the क्तवतु-कृदन्त (past active participle: -त-वत् stem) of √श्रु, प्रथमा एकवचन पुल्लिङ्ग — "having heard / one who has heard" — qualifying the implicit अहम्. एतत् गुह्यम् परम् — "this supreme secret."

18.75.title: साक्षात् कथयतः स्वयम् — directly, in person
18.75.body: योगेश्वरात् कृष्णात् (पञ्चमी, "from Kṛṣṇa, the lord of yoga") कथयतः (पञ्चमी एकवचन of शतृ-stem कथयत्, "from him as he was speaking") साक्षात् स्वयम् — "directly, in person." Sañjaya emphasizes the unmediated source. The participle agrees in case with कृष्णात्; पञ्चमी and षष्ठी of शतृ-stems are homophonous (-तः), but the syntactic anchor कृष्णात् fixes the case as ablative.

18.76.title: संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य — the reduplicating absolutive
18.76.body: The doubled absolutive संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य means "again and again recollecting." The repetition is grammatical, not stylistic decoration — Sanskrit reduplicates absolutives to indicate iterated action. Sañjaya is in a state of recurring recall.

18.76.title: हृष्यामि च मुहुर्मुहुः — the present-tense thrill
18.76.body: हृष्यामि (लट् उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन of √हृष्, "I thrill") मुहुर्मुहुः ("again and again"). पुण्यम् ("sacred") modifies संवादम्. Each remembering produces a fresh thrill — the past dialogue keeps acting on the present. केशवार्जुनयोः is षष्ठी द्विवचन — "of Keśava and Arjuna."

18.77.title: तत् च संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य — the second reduplication
18.77.body: Parallel to 18.76, but now the object is रूपम् अति-अद्भुतम् हरेः ("the supremely-marvelous form of Hari") — the viśvarūpa of chapter 11. The absolutive doubles again: Sañjaya re-experiences both the दिव्य संवाद and the दिव्य रूप.

18.77.title: विस्मयो मे महान् — wonder, mine, great
18.77.body: विस्मयः (प्रथमा, "wonder") मे (षष्ठी, "mine") महान् ("great") — three words, no verb needed (अस्ति implied). हृष्यामि च पुनः पुनः — the present-tense thrill is renewed. Sañjaya seals the rūpa-recall with the same hṛṣyāmi verb as the saṃvāda-recall.

18.78.title: यत्र-यत्र-तत्र — the locative trilogy of the seal
18.78.body: यत्र योगेश्वरः कृष्णः, यत्र पार्थः धनुर्धरः — two locative-where clauses naming the two presences. तत्र (correlative "there") names the result. The structure is ritual-like: where these two are co-present, there the listed goods follow.

18.78.title: श्रीः विजयः भूतिः ध्रुवा नीतिः — five outcomes, one verdict
18.78.body: Five प्रथमा एकवचन nouns — Śrī (prosperity), विजय (victory), भूति (well-being), ध्रुवा नीति (firm-fixed policy) — with the closing मतिः मम ("my conviction"). Sañjaya seals the entire Gītā with a personal verdict: this is his मति, his settled judgment. The chapter, and the text, close on a single first-person possessive.
